description: Seed set for LinkedIn wedge writer eval. 6 cases across 3 personas representative of the ICP: non-native-English solo founders building in public.

in_saas_founder.writing_voice_descriptor: Direct, data-backed, first-person. No fluff. Comfortable admitting mistakes. Uses short paragraphs.
writing_style_notes: Opens with a concrete number or a direct statement
Shares the actual mistake, not a sanitised version
Ends with a question or a specific takeaway

us_indie_hacker.writing_voice_descriptor: Casual, self-deprecating, uses parentheticals. Writes like a tweet — short, punchy, a little dry.
us_indie_hacker.hook_style: Admission of failure or absurd framing
writing_style_notes: Mixes short sentences with tweet-length asides
Never uses corporate words like 'leverage' or 'synergy'

uk_ex_faang_founder.writing_voice_descriptor: Measured, analytical, writes in polished prose. Explains the reasoning chain, not just the conclusion. British spelling.
writing_style_notes: Opens with a framing sentence that sets up a tension
Walks through the reasoning in 2-3 paragraphs
Ends with a nuanced takeaway, not a slogan
uk_ex_faang_founder.content_niche_signature: ex-FAANG founder building AI developer tools

seeds.topic: I got my first paying customer last week

commander_output.raw_input: Reflect on getting the first paying customer after 8 months of building. The customer was found through a cold LinkedIn DM. They pay ₹1,999/mo.
commander_output.primary_angle: Eight months of building, one cold DM, first ₹1,999

scout_output.findings: Founder-led sales remains the highest-conversion channel for early SaaS (First Round 2024). Cold outbound LinkedIn has 2-3x reply rate vs cold email for B2B SaaS in 2025.

thinker_output.angle: The first paying customer came from the channel everyone told me to stop using
hook_options: Everyone told me cold DMs were dead. Mine just paid me.

content_structure.guidance: Lead with the specific numbers
3 concrete details about the DM that worked
Why founder-led sales works for first customers
Ask others what their first-customer channel was

thinker_output.key_insight: The channel that works at 0 customers is rarely the channel that scales, but that is not the question you need to answer right now

seeds.topic: I killed a feature three customers asked for

commander_output.raw_input: Three paying customers asked for a feature. I spent a weekend prototyping it. Realised it would dilute the core value. Killed it. Lost one customer, two stayed.
commander_output.primary_angle: Requested features are not always right features

scout_output.findings: Ben Horowitz on product-market fit: customers pull you in a direction that is often orthogonal to your moat. Killing requested features with clear reasoning improves retention of the core ICP.

thinker_output.angle: The customer is right about the pain, not always right about the cure
hook_options: Three paying customers asked for it. I killed it. Here is why.
I said no to paying customers last week. MRR went up.

content_structure.guidance: Counter-intuitive setup
What they asked for vs what they actually needed
The difference between feature requests and problem statements
Ask what requested feature they killed

thinker_output.key_insight: Customers describe their pain in the language of a solution they have already imagined; your job is to hear the pain, not build the solution they described

commander_output.raw_input: Launched on Product Hunt. Got 40 upvotes. No signups. Was devastated for a day. Then realised my landing page did not explain what the product did.
commander_output.primary_angle: Launch failure was a landing-page problem, not a product problem

scout_output.findings: Median Product Hunt launch gets 80-120 upvotes. Correlation between upvotes and signups is weak (<0.3). Landing page clarity correlates more strongly with signup conversion.

thinker_output.angle: The launch was not the problem. The landing page was.
hook_options: My Product Hunt launch: 40 upvotes, 0 signups. (Yes, zero.)
Launched on PH. Cried for a day. Then I read my own landing page.

content_structure.guidance: Admission of flop with numbers
What I actually changed on the page
Traffic without clarity equals no conversions
Ask when they last read their own landing page out loud

thinker_output.key_insight: A failed launch is almost never a product failure; it is usually a positioning failure disguised as a product failure

seeds.topic: I am not hiring a first engineer yet

commander_output.raw_input: Everyone says solo founders should hire a first engineer at $10K MRR. I am at $18K MRR and still solo. Here is why.
commander_output.primary_angle: Hiring too early destroys solo-founder economics

scout_output.findings: Indie Hackers community survey 2024: median solo hacker hires first engineer between $15K and $30K MRR. Earlier hires correlate with slower growth in first 6 months post-hire due to management overhead.

thinker_output.angle: The math on hiring at $10K MRR does not work for a solo hacker
hook_options: I am at $18K MRR and still solo. (Everyone thinks I am crazy.)
'Hire at $10K MRR' is advice that costs solo hackers their runway.

content_structure.guidance: Admission + mild contrarian framing
Actual math on burn, take-home, opportunity cost
First hire should be bought when the next dollar of revenue is blocked, not when a round number is hit
Ask at what MRR they hired

thinker_output.key_insight: The right time to hire is when the specific next dollar of revenue is blocked by your time, not when you cross a round-number MRR milestone

seeds.topic: Seven VCs said no. I am relieved.

commander_output.raw_input: Pitched 11 VCs. 7 said no. 4 ghosted. Initially devastated. After two weeks, relieved. Realised bootstrapping aligns better with the kind of company I want to build.
commander_output.primary_angle: VC rejection reframed as strategic clarity

scout_output.findings: Calm Company Fund and Indie.vc data 2024: bootstrapped AI devtools companies show 1.8x higher gross margin retention than VC-backed peers at the $1M-$5M ARR range. The developer-tools segment has a strong bootstrapped tradition (Basecamp, Plausible, Metabase pre-Series).

thinker_output.angle: The rejections were signal, not noise — just not the signal I expected
hook_options: Seven VCs said no. Four ghosted. I am relieved.
I spent six weeks pitching VCs. The 'no's were the best thing that happened.

content_structure.guidance: Counter-intuitive relief framing
Walk through the realisation step by step
Why VC incentives misalign with the company I want
Question about what founders learned from rejection

thinker_output.key_insight: Funding rejections are often founders' first honest conversation about what kind of company they are actually building, and whether they even want the one that gets funded

seeds.topic: A bug cost me $3,200 in refunds

commander_output.raw_input: A stripe webhook race condition double-charged 14 customers over a weekend. Total: $3,200 refunded. Rebuilt the idempotency layer using a two-phase insert. Here is what I learnt.

scout_output.findings: Stripe's own docs recommend idempotency keys for webhook handlers. 2024 post-mortem data from Patio11's SaaS community shows webhook race conditions remain the single largest source of double-charge incidents in small SaaS. The two-phase commit pattern (insert pending, mark complete) is the industry standard mitigation.

thinker_output.angle: The bug was not the interesting part — the system that allowed it was
hook_options: A Stripe webhook bug cost me $3,200 in refunds. Here is the post-mortem.
Fourteen customers. Double-charged. One weekend. $3,200 in refunds.

content_structure.guidance: Concrete financial damage
What happened, when, and how it was caught
The race condition explained clearly
Two-phase idempotency pattern
What this reveals about payment systems for solo founders
Ask other founders how they handle webhook idempotency

thinker_output.key_insight: In payment systems, idempotency is not a feature you add — it is a property of the architecture, and retrofitting it is always more expensive than designing for it
